translations.translation:
Fei Yi (Wenwei)
費禕 (文偉)
Lived AD ?–253
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
Scroll 44 (Shu 14)
byZL181
onMarch 29th, 2025

Fei Yi, styledWenwei, was fromMeng[鄳],Jiangxia[江夏].A
Meng [鄳] is pronounced Mang [盲].
When he was young, he was orphaned, so he was reliant on the father of his clansman,[Fei] Boren. The paternal aunt of[Fei] Borenwas the mother of theGovernorofYi province[益州牧],Liu Zhang.[Liu] Zhangsent a messenger to receive[Fei] [Bo]ren, and[Fei] [Bo]renled[Fei] Yito study in Shu.
When theFormer Lordtook over Shu [蜀],[Fei] Yiremained in the lands ofYi[益]. He,Xu ShulongofRunan[汝南], andDong YunofNan commandery[南郡] were of equal fame. When the son ofXu Jingdied,[Dong] Yunand[Fei] Yiwanted to meet together at the burial place.[Dong] Yunreported this to his father,[Dong] He, and asked for a chariot.[Dong] Hegave a cart driven by deer with the back open.[Dong] Yunlooked like he was unwilling to be on the ride, but[Fei] Yireadily went in it first. When they were at the burial place,Zhuge Liangand other noble men were all gathered, and they had chariots that were very gorgeous.[Dong] Yunstill had an expression of discomfort, but[Fei] Yilooked relaxed and acted naturally. When the chariot handler returned,[Dong] Hequestioned him, and thus knew about all of such. He then told[Dong] Yun, “I often suspected that you andWenweiwere no different in your strengths and weaknesses, but now and hereafter, my opinion of such has been settled.”
When theFormer Lordestablished theHeir Apparent,[Fei] Yiand[Dong] Yunboth served asStaff Members, then promoted to Cadets, and after theLatter Lordascended the throne, they served asAttending Gentlemen at the Yellow Gates.
WhenAssisting Chancellor[Zhuge] Liangreturned from his expedition against the south, the officials, lining tens of miles, greeted and received him. Many of their years and positions were superior relative to[Fei] Yi, but[Zhuge] Liangspecially ordered[Fei] Yito ride together with him. Hence, there were none among the masses who did not change their views.
[Zhuge] Liang, because he had just returned from the south, appointed[Fei] Yito serve asColonel of Manifest Trustand was sent toWuas an envoy.[Sun] Quanwas comedic and witty by nature and his mocking banter was limitless. The likes ofZhuge KeandYang Daowere talented, rich [in knowledge], decisive, and persuasive, and their arguments and rebuttals were very sharp.[Fei] Yipossessed mild rhetoric and sincere sentiments, his replies based on logic. To the end, he was unable to be subdued.B[Sun] Quanhighly valued this and told[Fei] Yi, “Sir, you are a man of worthiness and virtue within this world. You will certainly serve in a critical capacity for the court ofShu, and I fear that you would then be unable to make any morevisits!”C
TheSeparate Biography of Fei Yistates:[Sun] Quanwould always personally pour good wine to treat[Fei] Yiand observe him being drunk. He would then ask him about national affairs in addition to talking about current affairs. Many difficult words would pile one after another.[Fei] Yiwould always excuse himself as being intoxicated, and when he withdrew, he would organize what was asked of him. He would then answer each matter with no mistakes.
TheSeparate Biography of Fei Yistates:[Sun] Quanhad a bejeweled blade he often had on hand gifted to him.[Fei] Yireplied, “I am of no talent. How could I bear such a sagacious order? Nevertheless, blades are the means to punishing the disobedient1and prohibiting the disorderly. I only hope thatYour Majestywill work hard towards establishing a legacy of accomplishment and join us in aiding theHan dynasty. Even though I am ignorant and weak, I would never fail to betray the wishes of theeast.”•••
Here, “disobedient” here literally means “not attending court” [不庭]. To not attend court was a sign of defiance.↩
When he returned, he was promoted to bePalace Attendant. When[Zhuge] Liangwent north to station atHanzhong[漢中], he invited[Fei] Yito serve asMilitary Adjutant. Because he as an envoy fulfilled wishes, he frequently went toWu.
In the eighth year of theera [230], he was transferred to serve asCentral Army Protector, and he later also served asMajor. It happened thatDirector GeneralWei YanandChief ClerkYang Yidespised each other. Every time they were seated with each other, they would argue with each other.[Wei] Yanwould sometimes brandish his blade and direct it towards[Yang] Yi, and[Yang] Yiwould shed tears and mucus that mixed with each other. Whenever[Fei] Yiwent between their seats, he would make explanations and divide them. This would last to the end of the time of[Zhuge] Liang. Every time[Wei] Yanand[Yang] Yiwere used to their fullest, it would be due to the efforts of[Fei] Yiwho would correct [their bad behavior] and rescue [them from wicked deeds].
When[Zhuge] Liangdied,[Fei] Yibecame RearDirector General. Soon after, he replacedJiang WanasDirector of the Secretariat.DWhen[Jiang] Wanreturned toFu[涪] fromHanzhong[漢中],[Fei] Yiwas promoted toGrand GeneralandAuthority over the Secretariat.
TheSeparate Biography of Fei Yistates: At this time, there were many military and national concerns, and the official matters were disorganized and overwhelming. The discernment and understanding[Fei] Yihad surpassed others. Every time he inspected or recited messages or records, he could raise his eyes to gaze upon them for a short time and he would already grasp its meanings; his swiftness in such was more than twice of others, and he would also never forget them even to the end. From morning to evening, he would often listen to concerns. In the meantime, he would receive guests, and he would drink, eat, and have fun with them, and he would also play Go. He would always fulfill the pleasures of people, but the concerns would also not be neglected. WhenDong Yunreplaced[Fei] YiasDirector of the Secretariat, he wished to emulate what[Fei] Yicould do, but within ten days, many concerns were delayed.[Dong] Yunthus sighed saying, “The talents and strengths of people can be separate from each other in such a distant way! This is beyond my level! I can listen to concerns all day long, but I still have no free time!”
In the seventh year of theera [244], the army ofWeiwent toXingshi[興勢], so[Fei] Yiwas givenacting tallyand led the armies to resist them. TheBrilliantly Blessed Grandee,Lai Min, arrived at where[Fei] Yiwas to bid farewell, and sought to play Go together. At the time, the feathered dispatches2were being rushed constantly, men and horses wore armor, and the preparation of the chariots had finished.[Fei] Yiand[Lai] Minwere concentrated and replied to each other with banter, their expressions being without any fatigue.[Lai] Minthus said, “Just now, I was observing and testing you, sir! You, sir, are truly the right person. You will surely be able to do something about the traitors.” When[Fei] Yicame, the traitors retreated. He was thus enfeoffed asTownship Marquisof Cheng [成鄉侯].E•••
Feathered dispatches indicate documents of high urgency and the recipient should look at them as soon as possible.↩
TheCoherent SayingsbyYin Jistates: WhenSima YiexecutedCao Shuang,[Fei] Yiset forth view one and view two to discuss its properness. View one believed that “Cao Shuangand his brothers were all ordinary fellows, and it was because they were scions of the imperial clan that they were allowed to bear the responsibility of the parting command.3They, however, acted with conceit and extravagance, and they exceeded their authority. They associated with unbecoming men and they cultivated a clique of their comrades for themselves, seeking to throw the nation into chaos.[Sima] Yipunished them with vigor, exterminating them all in one morning. Thus, it is this act that should be praised for in his duty, for he supplemented the hopes of the masses.” View two believed that “[Sima] Yiwas resentful thatCao Zhong4had entrusted to him besides others. How could he and[Cao] Shuangmeddle with each other?5The state of affairs was not concentrated and he used that to ferment faults in secret. From the beginning, he did not offer any loyal words or candid instructions, and then he slew them all in one morning, slandering them of even the unexpected. How could this be the deed of a respectful man who manages the nation and strengthens the roots? If[Cao] Shuangtruly had the mindset of plotting against his liege, his high treason would have already been prepared. But on the day troops were dispatched,[Cao] Fangwas readily entrusted to[Cao] Shuangand his brothers. Following that,[Sima] Yiand his sons closed the city gates and mustered troops, then closed in on[Cao] Fang, which guaranteed that there would be no peace whatsoever. How could that be said to be a loyal minister acting with prudence for his lord? Deducing off this,[Cao] Shuangbeing without great misdeeds is clear. If[Sima] Yibelieved that[Cao] Shuangwas extravagant and exceeding his duties, then to remove him and to punish him would be proper. But he exterminated even his infant relatives with the claim of them being wickedness and ended the blood sacrifices toZidan.6The son ofHe Yan, who was a maternal nephew ofWei, also befell harm. He thus exceeded his duties and actedinappropriately!”•••
A “parting command” [顧命] is the final wish of an emperor before they died.Cao Shuangwas given the responsibility of serving as regent by the emperor.↩
Cao Ruihad the style name ofYuanzhong[元仲]. It is unknown why he is called Cao Zhong here. This could be the simple error of the “Yuan” not being copied. Less likely, Cao Zhong could mean “second Cao” in reference to him being the second emperor of theWei.↩
This is basically saying “how could two powerful and ambitious people meddle with each other”, suggesting that one would eventually eliminate the over.↩
Descendants would often sacrifice animals to their ancestors. If there are no descendants who could sacrifice, the blood sacrifices are considered to have been ended.↩
When[Jiang] Wanfirmly yielded his provincial duties,[Fei] YibecameInspectorofYi province[益州刺史]. The achievements and notable deeds[Fei] Yiaccomplished in managing the nation were generally comparable to those of[Jiang] Wan.F
TheSeparate Biography of Fei Yistates:[Fei] Yihad a noble nature: he was modest and plain, his family not having excess wealth. His sons were all made to wear plain clothes and eat vegetarian meals, and whenever they left or entered [a place], they would not be followed by chariots or cavalry. They were no different from ordinary people.
In the eleventh year [248], he marched toHanzhong[漢中]. Even though[Jiang] Wanand[Fei] Yiwere physically outside [of the imperial court], any commendations or punishments would first be [sent] afar to be consulted and decided by them before they were enacted, receiving trust and responsibility in such a manner. Later, in the fourteenth year [251], summer, he returned toChengdu[成都]. People inChengdu[成都] who observed the skies said that the capital city would be without the position of chancellor. Hence, in the winter, he returned to station atHanshou[漢壽].
In the fifteenth year of theera [252],[Fei] Yiwas given his own bureau. In the sixteenth year [253], there was a major assembly on the first day of the year. A surrenderee from theWei,Guo Xun, was in attendance.[Fei] Yihad merrily drunk and was deeply intoxicated. He then was slain by the blade of[Guo] Xun. His posthumous title was theRespectedMarquis[敬侯].
***
His son,[Fei] Cheng, succeeded him. He served asAttending Gentleman at the Yellow Gates. The younger brother of[Fei] Chengwas[Fei] Gong, who married a princess.GThe eldest daughter of[Fei] Yiwas matched with theHeir Apparent,[Liu] Xuan, to be hisConsort.
TheSeparate Biography of Fei Yistates:[Fei] Gongserved asGentleman of the Secretariat. He had a famous reputation in his era, but he died early.
translations.page_title: Fei Yi (Wenwei) 費禕 (文偉) Sanguozhi Biography by ZL181 - Records of the Three Kingdoms (三國志) by Chen Shou – Kongming’s Archives

Fei Yi (Wenwei)
費禕 (文偉)
Lived AD ?–253

in2004

Fei Yi, styled Wenwei, hailed from Meng, Jiangxia. Orphaned at a young age, he lived with one Boren, an elder of his clan. Boren’s father’s sister was the mother of Liu Zhang, Governor of Yizhou.
Liu Zhang sent an envoy to bring Boren [into Shu], and Boren took Fei Yi along to Shu for his education. At that time, the First Sovereign [Liu Bei] took over Shu, and Fei Yi remained in Yi province. He became of the same fame as Xu Shulong of Runan and Dong Yun of Nanjun.
On one occasion, Xu Jing’s son died, and Dong Yun and Fei Yi wished to attend the funeral. Dong Yun asked his father, Dong He, for a carriage to take them there, but Dong He only gave him an open-back rustic rickshaw. Dong Yun’s unwillingness to ride this cart showed immediately on his face, but Fei Yi went ahead and got on the cart. When they arrived at the place of the funeral, they saw that Zhuge Liang and other noble people were there already with their splendid carriages. Dong Yun still looked uneasy, but Fei Yi was untroubled and acted as if all was normal. When the cart-pusher returned, Dong He asked him [about what happened] and found out about this. Thus he said to Dong Yun, “I had often wondered which of you and Wenwei is better of character, but from now on, I know for sure.”
When the First Sovereign announced his heir, Fei Yi and Dong Yun were both made guards of the crown prince, and were in time promoted to be attendants in the prince’s office. Fei Yi was made Gentleman in Attendance of the Yellow Gates, when the Latter Sovereign [Liu Shan] became emperor. When Prime Minister Zhuge Liang returned from the southern expedition, the officers all went to welcome him tens oflioutside the city, and most of them were senior to Fei Yi either in terms of age or in rank. However, Zhuge Liang especially commanded Fei Yi to ride in the carriage with him. Everyone changed their opinion of Fei Yi after that.
Because he had just returned from the south, Zhuge Liang made Fei Yi Colonel who Illustrates Trustworthiness and sent him as an ambassador to Wu. Sun Quan was facetious of nature and his glib tongue knew no limits, and people like Zhuge Ke and Yang Dao were well-learned and keen debaters. They posed difficult discussion topics and pointed remarks, but Fei Yi was able to answer with elegant words and good arguments, and responded with good logic and reason. Not once was he overwhelmed.ASun Quan considered very highly of him, and said to him thus, “Sir, you are one of the most upright and virtuous man of the world. As thus, you will certainly become an indispensable minister of the Shu kingdom, and I fear that you would not have the opportunity to visit veryoften!”B
Supplementary biography of Yi: Sun Quan would always arrange to have the best wine served to Fei Yi, and wait for him to be drunk before asking him about administrative business, and starting a discussion on current affairs. [Sun Quan] would discuss difficult topics one after another. Every time, Fei Yi would decline to answer, and excusing himself on the account of being drunk, he would return to his place of abode and write down replies to the questions one by one, omitting nothing.
Supplementary biography of Yi: And so Sun Quan took the precious blade that he often kept in his hand, and gave it to [Fei Yi] as a gift. Yi replied, “I, your servant, am untalented—how can I deserve to be entrusted to let your command be known? However, this blade will be used to punish those who are insubordinate and to subdue rebels. My only wish is that Your Majesty will continue to accomplish great things, and to uphold the virtues of the Han. Though I be dull and weak, I will not let down the expectations the East holds for me.”
At his return Fei Yi was promoted to be an honourary palace attendant. When Zhuge Liang was in Hanzhong in the north, he requested for Fei Yi to be an advisor in his office [in Chengdu]. Fei Yi was also sent as an envoy to Wu on numerous occasions because of his ability to complete his mission satisfactorily. In the 8th year of Jianxing (AD 230), he was made Central Army Commissioner, and later became a major. At that time, army advisor Wei Yan and chief clerk Yang Yi hated each other, and every time they meet they ended up in an altercation. Sometimes Wei Yan would draw his sword and wave it at Yang Yi, and Yang Yi would weep freely. When this happens, Fei Yi would position himself between their seats, and separate them with admonishments. The fact that Wei Yan and Yang Yi’s talents were able to be used to their fullest during Zhuge Liang’s time was due to Fei Yi’s efforts in saving [their relationship]. After Zhuge Liang died, Fei Yi was made Army Advisor of the Rear. Later on, he replaced Jiang Wan as the Head of the Imperial Secretarial Office.CWhen Jiang Wan returned to Fu from Hanzhong, Fei Yi was promoted to General-in-Chief and given full control of the Imperial Secretarial Office.
Supplementary biography of Yi: At that time, many things were happening both inside and outside the borders, and the offices were bogged down with numerous administrative affairs. Fei Yi, having an extraordinary memory, needed only to skim through the documents and reports. He only needed to look briefly at something to understand the ideas in it. He read many times the speed of normal people, and neither would he forget what he read. Most of the time, he would attend to official business only in early morning and late afternoon, and during the time in between he would receive guests, feast and play, gamble and play chess, enjoying all the good things in life. Though this is so, he has never neglected any piece of his work. When Dong Yun replaced Fei Yi as Head of the Imperial Secretarial Office, he tried to imitate Fei Yi’s lifestyle. However, within ten days, a backlog of work built up. At this, Dong Yun sighed and said, “What a difference there can be in the talents of each person! I can never attain his level. Though I busy myself with official business all day, I still cannot attend to everything.”
In the 7th year of Yanxi (AD 244), the Wei army approached Xingshi. Fei Yi was given thejieauthority and sent to defend against them with a force. Lai Min, Grandee of the Imperial Household, came to see Fei Yi off, but invited Fei Yi for a game of Weiqi. Although feathered urgent documents1were being brought in and sent out non-stop, the troops were all in full armour, and Fei Yi’s own carriage was ready to go, Fei Yi played the game with Lai Min most attentively and showed neither anxiety nor tiredness in his face. Lai Min said, “I was just testing your reaction! You are indeed a trustworthy man and the one who can crush the invaders.” Once Fei Yi arrived [at Hanzhong], the enemy retreated. For that he was granted the title of Marquis of Chengxiang.D•••
Feathers were pasted onto the envelope of a document to signify its urgency.↩
Yan Ji’sTongyusays: When Sima Yi had Cao Shuang killed, Fei Yi presented the following argumentation to reason out who was right and who was wrong in the matter. Viewpoint A assumes that Cao Shuang and his brothers were men of ordinary caliber, and only because they were scions of the imperial house were they given the responsibility of advising the [young emperor]. However, they were arrogant and assumed luxuries and power beyond their station, befriending improper people, building up their own faction to plot against the kingdom. Therefore, when Sima Yi led a force against them in punitive action and eliminated the lot at once, he showed that he was worthy of the task entrusted to him and his action was in accordance with the hopes and wishes of the people.
Viewpoint B takes Sima Yi to be discontent at the former Cao lord’s not giving power solely to him, as well as Cao Shuang’s interfering with his business. Shishibu zhuan, and thus he secretly harboured a grudge against him. Therefore, he did not give Cao Shuang’s group admonishment or warning beforehand, and on one day slaughtered them all, taking them off guard. In this case, he was not doing something that a gentleman would do to preserve the kingdom.
Now, if we assume Cao Shuang had been plotting against his lord, and his treasonous plans were already in motion. On the day the mutiny happened, Cao Fang was in the hands of Cao Shuang and his brother. When Sima Yi and his sons put a force together behind closed gates and turn it in Cao Fang’s direction, Cao Fang’s safety was surely compromised. Can this really be a loyal minister acting for the good of his lord? Reasoning from this, it is clear that Cao Shuang was not guilty of great evils. If Sima Yi really considered Cao Shuang to be guilty of extravagance and arrogance, it would suffice for him to execute him according to the law. However, he exterminated even his infant children, branding them with the name of disloyalty, effectively wiping out Zidan’s [Cao Shuang’s] line. Also, He Yan’s son was a nephew of the Wei ruler, and even he was killed. Sima Yi was assuming too much power and behaving improperly.
Jiang Wan resolutely resigned from his provincial administrative duties and passed them on to Fei Yi, and Fei Yi took on the post of Governor of Yizhou [on top of his original post]. The accomplishments and fame Fei Yi was equal to none but Jiang Wan in the kingdom.EIn the 11th year (AD 248), he garrisoned in Hanzhong. When Jiang Wan and Fei Yi were stationed outside the capital, [Liu Shan] would ask for their opinion for all things pertaining to awards, promotions, punishments, or demotions, before proceeding with those actions. This was an indication of how much he trusted them.
Supplementary biography of Yi: Fei Yi had the virtue of being humble and living simply, and gathered no wealth in his household. He had his children dress in simple cloth and eat of plain foods. No mounts nor carriages were to follow them when they went about. In everything they were as the commoners were.
Later on, in the summer of the 14th year (AD 251), he returned to Chengdu. Diviners in Chengdu said that there would be no position for a prime minister in the capital city, and thus Fei Yi returned north to Hanshou during the winter. In the 15th year of Yanxi (AD 252), [the court] commanded Fei Yi to establish his own office. On new year’s day of the 16th year, Fei Yi held a party, and among the guests was Guo Xun, who had defected from Wei. Fei Yi drank freely in merriment, and when he was quite intoxicated, Guo Xun stabbed him to death.
Fei Yi was given the posthumous title of Respectful Marquis, and his son Fei Cheng was his heir and was made Gentleman in Attendance of the Yellow Gates. Fei Cheng’s younger brother, Fei Gong, was married to a princess.FFei Yi’s eldest daughter was made a consort of Crown Prince Xuan.
Supplementary biography of Yi: Fei Gong became an attendant in the Imperial Secretarial Office, and his name was know to all in the land. He died at a young age.
